narrative: As a white male applicant with a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic and personal factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 24 (well under the average 36.70), Year 1 grade decile of 4 and Year 3 decile of 2 (both lower than school averages around 5-6), and your plan to attend full-time—and calculated a 50.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This borderline-high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. Among all the factors in your profile, three stood out as having the strongest influence on pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction. Your LSAT score was the top driver, as its low value significantly increased the predicted risk of bar failure compared to what the model sees in stronger applicants. Next, your Year 3 grade decile played a key role in the same direction, signaling weaker late-undergrad performance relative to peers, which the model associates with bar struggles. Interestingly, your family income bracket had the strongest counterbalancing effect, nudging the prediction toward success more than any other factor—likely because the model recognizes that students from similar backgrounds can overcome odds with determination. Overall, your academics fell short of typical successful students, whose higher LSATs and grade deciles dominate bar-passing profiles, tipping the scales against you despite the positive income signal. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations have succeeded with targeted effort. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 35 or higher, strengthening your recent grades through post-bac courses or a master's program, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Reapply after these improvements, perhaps to schools with holistic reviews that weigh your full story, and consider bar prep resources early to build confidence. With deliberate steps, you can shift your profile into the "pass" territory the model favors.
